questions.question_text: What is the default form of co-ownership when no specific form is stated in a Connecticut deed?
questions.explanation: Tenancy in common is the default form of co-ownership in Connecticut. To create joint tenancy, the deed must explicitly state that intent. Community property does not exist in Connecticut.

questions.question_text: A married couple in Connecticut wants co-ownership with right of survivorship where neither can sell without the other's consent. They should hold title as:
questions.explanation: Tenancy by the entirety is available only to married couples in Connecticut and provides right of survivorship plus protection from unilateral transfer. Joint tenancy has survivorship but allows unilateral transfer.

questions.question_text: To create a joint tenancy in Connecticut, the deed must:
questions.correct_answer: Explicitly state joint tenancy with right of survivorship
distractors: Simply list both names on the deed
Be recorded within 30 days of execution
questions.explanation: Connecticut requires explicit language creating joint tenancy with right of survivorship. Without such language, co-ownership defaults to tenancy in common. The four unities (time, title, interest, possession) must also be present.

questions.question_text: Connecticut recognizes which forms of property ownership? (Select the correct combination)
questions.correct_answer: Tenancy in common, joint tenancy, tenancy by entirety
distractors: Only tenancy in common and joint tenancy
Tenancy in common, joint tenancy, and community property
Only tenancy in common and tenancy by entirety
questions.explanation: Connecticut recognizes tenancy in common, joint tenancy, and tenancy by the entirety. Connecticut does NOT have community property — that system exists only in a handful of states like California and Texas.

questions.question_text: Two business partners own a commercial building as tenants in common. One partner dies. The deceased partner's share:
questions.correct_answer: Passes to the deceased partner's heirs or estate
distractors: Automatically transfers to the surviving partner
Reverts to the state of Connecticut
Is split equally among all remaining tenants
questions.explanation: Tenancy in common has no right of survivorship. When a tenant in common dies, their share passes through their estate to heirs. Only joint tenancy and tenancy by the entirety have survivorship rights.

questions.question_text: What is the statutory period for adverse possession in Connecticut?
questions.explanation: Under CGS §52-575, the statutory period for adverse possession in Connecticut is 15 years of continuous, open, notorious, hostile, and exclusive possession.

questions.question_text: A neighbor has been using a path across another's property openly and without permission for 16 years. This may establish a:
questions.explanation: In Connecticut, a prescriptive easement requires 15 years of open, continuous, and hostile use without permission. At 16 years, the neighbor may have established a prescriptive easement over the path.

questions.question_text: The prescriptive easement period in Connecticut is:
questions.correct_answer: The same as adverse possession — 15 years
questions.explanation: Both adverse possession and prescriptive easements in Connecticut require 15 years. The use must be open, visible, continuous, and without the owner's permission for the full period.

questions.question_text: To claim adverse possession in Connecticut, the possession must be all of the following EXCEPT:
distractors: Open and notorious to the public
Exclusive and hostile to the owner
questions.explanation: Adverse possession requires possession that is WITHOUT the owner's consent (hostile). If the owner granted written permission, the possession is not hostile and cannot support an adverse possession claim.

questions.question_text: A property owner discovers a neighbor's fence has been 3 feet over the boundary line for 12 years. The owner should:
questions.correct_answer: Take action now before the 15-year period expires
distractors: Wait because 20 years is needed for adverse possession
Do nothing because fences don't count for adverse claims
File a lawsuit only if the fence was built intentionally
questions.explanation: With only 3 years remaining before the 15-year adverse possession period is met, the owner should act promptly. After 15 years of open, continuous, hostile possession, the neighbor could claim title to the strip.

questions.question_text: Connecticut uses what type of recording system for land records?
questions.explanation: Connecticut uses a town clerk recording system where each of the 169 municipalities maintains its own land records. Documents must be recorded in the town where the property is located.

questions.question_text: A buyer is purchasing property that borders two different towns. Where should the deed be recorded?
questions.correct_answer: In each town where a portion of the property is located
distractors: Only in the town where the main structure sits
At the county recorder's office for that region
At the centralized Connecticut land records office
questions.explanation: Connecticut uses a town-based (not county-based) recording system. When property spans multiple towns, the deed must be recorded in each town's land records to provide constructive notice in all relevant jurisdictions.

questions.question_text: Connecticut follows which type of recording statute?
questions.explanation: Connecticut follows a race-notice recording statute. A subsequent purchaser who records first and had no notice of a prior unrecorded interest prevails. Both good faith AND recording first are required.

questions.question_text: Buyer A purchases property but does not record the deed. Buyer B later purchases the same property without knowledge of Buyer A's deed and records immediately. Under Connecticut law:
questions.correct_answer: Buyer B prevails as a good-faith purchaser who recorded first
distractors: Buyer A prevails because they purchased first
Neither prevails until a court decides
Buyer B prevails only if they paid more
questions.explanation: Under Connecticut's race-notice statute, Buyer B prevails because they (1) had no notice of Buyer A's unrecorded interest and (2) recorded first. Both conditions must be met.

questions.question_text: Recording a deed in Connecticut provides what type of notice to the public?
distractors: No notice until a search is performed
questions.explanation: Recording a document gives constructive notice to the world — everyone is deemed to know about it whether they actually searched the records or not. This is a core principle of the recording system.
